When the FAR has not been maintained throughout the year, reconstructing it takes 3-4 weeks, delays audit sign-off, and almost always results in prior-period restatements of depreciation. Construction-in-progress amounts linger on the balance sheet after project completion because the capitalisation transfer to fixed assets is not triggered. MANIFESTS AS: auditor-required prior-period restatements of accumulated depreciation; overstated expenses in years when capital items are expensed; understated fixed asset base on balance sheet; incorrect fixed asset and depreciation disclosures in statutory financial statement notes; BIR straight-line depreciation claims disallowed due to wrong asset categories; FAR net book value reconciling differences to GL that run to PHP 500k–2M at year-end.

painPoints.label: Accruals Register & Prepayment Amortisation Breakdown
painPoints.description:
INCLUDES: identifying all recurring and non-recurring accruals required at period-end (utility estimates, accrued salaries for partially-worked periods, leave liability accruals, bonus accruals, accrued audit and professional fees, BIR penalty provisions, accrued interest on borrowings); posting accrual journal entries and ensuring corresponding reversal entries are set up for the following period; maintaining a structured accruals register that tracks each accrual by campus, account code, amount, basis of estimate, and reversal date; identifying and amortising prepaid expenses over their correct benefit periods (school insurance premiums, software licence fees prepaid annually, advance rental payments, prepaid maintenance contracts); maintaining a prepayment amortisation schedule reconciled to the prepayments asset balance in GL; detecting and correcting double-posted accruals and un-reversed prior-period accruals. EXCLUDES: payroll accruals for unpaid salaries (Payroll Processing), government grant accruals (Government Funding), revenue cut-off and deferred tuition revenue recognition (Billing & Collection), depreciation computation (fixed-asset-register node).

WHY IT HURTS: Monthly accruals across a 15-campus operator involve dozens of line items — utilities at each campus, insurance prepayments, deferred rental, contractual staff costs that straddle pay periods, audit fee accruals, and provisions for contingent BIR liabilities. These are typically maintained in separate Excel spreadsheets by different staff members with no version control and no structured reversal tracking. Reversals are missed: an accrual for December utility costs is not reversed in January, so the expense is recognised twice when the actual invoice arrives. Prepayment amortisation schedules fall out of sync with GL when asset accounts are not updated monthly; at year-end, the prepayments balance is materially overstated because several items have expired. Across 15 campuses, the cumulative impact of missed reversals and stale prepayments can exceed PHP 2-5M in misstated expenses and assets — significant enough to require audit adjustment. The problem compounds over time because prior-period errors are carried forward into base figures. MANIFESTS AS: month-on-month P&L volatility caused by erratic accrual posting rather than real operational changes; audit adjustments to correct over-accrued expenses and overstated prepayments; management accounts that are unreliable for decision-making because accrual completeness varies month to month; close process extended by unstructured accruals assembly from email threads and informal notes each month.

painPoints.label: Fragmented Submission Tracking Across Agencies
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: tracking outbound submissions to DepEd, BIR, PSA, PRC, CHED, TESDA, LGUs across all campuses; unified deadline calendar; submission status per agency per filing cycle; duplicate and missed filing detection; multi-campus deadline visibility for the compliance officer. EXCLUDES: actual submission preparation (Finance & Revenue for financial data; Workforce Management for HR data), accreditation submissions (Accreditation Management), data entry into agency portals.

painPoints.label: Manual Data Consolidation from Campuses
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: aggregating current-period student enrollment, headcount, and financial data from 10–15 campuses into institution-wide figures required for DepEd, BIR, SSS, PhilHealth, and CHED submissions. Pain includes: re-keying from SIS exports, mismatched field definitions across campuses, version control on consolidation spreadsheets, HQ consolidation taking 3–5 days per cycle. EXCLUDES: financial statement preparation, SIS administration, payroll data preparation.

painPoints.label: Incomplete or Lost Historical Records
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: inability to locate or verify 3–5 years of historical data (enrollment trends, fee structures, disciplinary records, scholarship registers) when regulatory agencies request them; pain caused by decentralised storage, staff turnover, and absence of document retention policy. EXCLUDES: financial records (Finance & Revenue), employee records (Workforce Management), academic transcripts (Teaching & Learning / Data & MIS).

painPoints.label: Format and Requirement Drift Across Agency Updates
painPoints.description: DepEd, BIR, and other agencies periodically update submission formats, data definitions, and interpretations. Multi-site teams miss these updates or misinterpret them. Pain point: a submission that passed last year fails this year due to format change, triggering rejection and re-work. EXCLUDES: monitoring of academic policy changes (Teaching & Learning), tax law interpretation (Finance & Revenue).

painPoints.label: Penalty and Interest Escalation from Late Filings
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: financial penalties and compounding interest that accumulate specifically from missed or delayed regulatory filing deadlines — BIR late filing surcharges (25% + 20% interest p.a.), SSS/PhilHealth/Pag-IBIG employer contribution penalties, DepEd non-compliance notices leading to operating warnings. EXCLUDES: penalty negotiation (Finance & Revenue), accreditation loss penalties (Accreditation Management).

painPoints.label: Over-Reliance on External Accountants for Compliance
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: structural over-reliance on external accountants as the primary mechanism for regulatory filing compliance; consequences: high recurring cost (PHP 15k–40k per filing cycle), slow turnaround, loss of in-house compliance memory, inability to self-serve on routine queries. EXCLUDES: audit services (Finance & Revenue), tax advisory (Finance & Revenue).

painPoints.label: Agency Correspondence & Query Management
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: receiving, routing, and responding to inbound communications from regulatory agencies — deficiency notices, notice of discrepancy (NOD), assessment letters, query letters, demands for additional documentation, show-cause orders. Pain: letters are misrouted, response deadlines missed, responses drafted from scratch without institutional memory. EXCLUDES: formal legal defence (Finance/Legal), accreditation correspondence (Accreditation Management).

painPoints.label: Inter-Agency Data Consistency and Reconciliation
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: reconciling conflicting data across multiple regulatory agencies that classify the same students, employees, or transactions differently — e.g., DepEd vs PSA enrollment counts (late enrollees), BIR vs SSS employee counts (part-time vs regular classifications), CHED vs institutional SIS program enrolment totals. Pain: each agency has different cutoff dates, definitions, and data refresh schedules. EXCLUDES: SIS data governance (Data & MIS), financial reconciliation (Finance & Revenue).

painPoints.label: Fragmented Evidence Collection Across Campuses
painPoints.description: Accrediting bodies require documented evidence: curriculum maps, faculty qualifications, facility records, governance minutes, student outcome data. Across 10-15 campuses, this evidence is stored in different formats, systems, and locations. Pain: the accreditation coordinator at HQ must chase each campus 3-6 months before a visit. EXCLUDES: SSR narrative writing (SSR Authoring), SLO data gaps (SLO Assessment Data Gaps).

painPoints.label: Student Learning Outcomes (SLO) Assessment Data Gaps
painPoints.description: A specialized evidence gap: SLO assessment data requires active, ongoing collection over 3+ years before an accreditation visit — it cannot be reconstructed retroactively. Pain: operators start systematic SLO tracking only when an accreditation visit is imminent. By then, historical data cannot be created. EXCLUDES: SLO curriculum design (Teaching & Learning), assessment delivery (Teaching & Learning).

painPoints.label: Faculty Credential Compliance Across Levels
painPoints.description: Accreditors set minimum academic qualification standards: master's degree for senior faculty, terminal degree ratios for graduate programs. Multi-campus operators struggle to maintain an up-to-date credential matrix. Pain: credentials from visiting faculty, new hires, and CPD completions are not tracked centrally. EXCLUDES: CPD hour tracking (Teacher Registration & CPD), HR records (Workforce Management).

painPoints.label: Facility and Resource Audit Lag
painPoints.description: Accreditors inspect whether campuses meet academic resource adequacy standards: lab equipment ratios, library collection depth, classroom ratios. Multi-campus operators rely on manual facility inventories. Pain: inventory is out of date at time of inspection, exposing gaps that could have been remediated. EXCLUDES: facility maintenance (Facilities Management), equipment procurement (Finance/Procurement).

painPoints.label: Accreditor Interpretation Variance Across Programs
painPoints.description: Multiple accrediting bodies (PAASCU, PACUCOA, AACCUP, CHED Technical Panels) have differing interpretations of standards even for similar programs. Pain: operators running both PAASCU-accredited and AACCUP-registered programs must prepare different evidence packages for the same underlying data. EXCLUDES: curriculum design (Teaching & Learning), financial reporting standards (Finance & Revenue).

painPoints.label: Corrective Action Plan Execution and Follow-up
painPoints.description: After an accreditation audit, operators receive formal findings and must submit a CAP with named responsible parties, evidence deliverables, and deadlines. Pain: CAP execution is tracked in spreadsheets, ownership is unclear, deadlines slip, and evidence for closure is not collected until re-audit. EXCLUDES: inspection CAPs (Inspection Preparation & Response), internal audit CAPs (Internal Quality Audits).

painPoints.label: Self-Study Report Authoring and Coordination Burden
painPoints.description: Accrediting bodies require a Self-Study Report (SSR) — a structured document with evidence, narrative analysis, and self-evaluation against accreditation standards. Pain: SSR authoring takes 3-6 months of staff time, involves 20+ contributors, and is coordinated via email. EXCLUDES: evidence collection (Fragmented Evidence Collection), accreditation cycle planning (Cycle Calendar Management).

painPoints.label: Multi-Body Accreditation Cycle Calendar Gaps
painPoints.description: Multi-campus operators running multiple programs face a complex accreditation renewal calendar: PAASCU institutional (3-5 years), AACCUP (its own cycle), CHED Technical Panels per program. Pain: operators lose track of upcoming renewal windows, missing preparatory deadlines by months. EXCLUDES: individual filing deadlines (Regulatory Filings), inspection scheduling (Inspection Preparation).

painPoints.label: Teacher License Renewal Tracking and Reminders
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: tracking PRC license expiry dates and TDMS registration renewal cycles for all teaching staff across campuses; issuing renewal reminders at 90/60/30-day milestones; managing provisional renewal status. EXCLUDES: CPD hour requirements (CPD Hour Tracking), credential submission (Document Custody).

painPoints.label: CPD Hour Tracking and Verification Across Programs
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: aggregating CPD hours earned by each teacher across all program types (in-house workshops, DepEd trainings, conferences, online courses); verifying attestations submitted by teachers against accredited provider lists; surfacing compliance gaps before annual renewal cycle. EXCLUDES: CPD program design (Teaching & Learning), CPD provider vetting (Accredited Provider Vetting).

painPoints.label: Accredited CPD Provider Vetting and Sourcing
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: maintaining an up-to-date register of DepEd-approved CPD providers; verifying that specific CPD offerings are accredited and will count toward compliance thresholds; sourcing providers to fill identified CPD gaps. EXCLUDES: CPD hour tracking (CPD Hour Tracking), budget for CPD (Finance).

painPoints.label: TDMS Enrollment Data Sync and Reconciliation Errors
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: reconciling teacher data between internal systems (SIS, payroll, HR HRIS) and the DepEd TDMS database; identifying and correcting discrepancies in names, birth dates, qualification dates, license numbers. Pain: manual reconciliation across 500+ staff is error-prone and time-consuming before annual TDMS submission deadlines. EXCLUDES: SIS administration (Data & MIS), payroll administration (Finance).

painPoints.label: Specialized Credential Gaps (BED, Licensure Exams)
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: identifying teaching positions that require credentials beyond a basic PRC license (BED degree, subject-specific licensure, TLE/MAPEH/SHS-track certifications); tracking whether currently employed staff hold required credentials; flagging gaps for recruitment or CPD. EXCLUDES: credential tracking for accreditation (Faculty Credential Compliance), recruitment (Workforce Management).

painPoints.label: Compliance Status Visibility at Appraisal and Promotion Decisions
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: surfacing each teacher's current compliance status (license validity, CPD hour balance, outstanding credential gaps) to HR appraisers and approving managers at the point of performance review or promotion decision. Pain: compliance status is not integrated into HR workflows — promotions are approved for staff with lapsed licenses. EXCLUDES: performance rating itself (Workforce Management), payroll adjustments (Finance).

painPoints.label: Non-Teaching Licensed Staff Credential Compliance
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: tracking PRC licensure requirements and renewal cycles for non-teaching licensed professionals — guidance counselors (RA 9258), school nurses (RA 9173), school librarians, social workers. These are often overlooked in compliance tracking focused on teachers. EXCLUDES: teacher license tracking (License Renewal Tracking), HR records (Workforce Management).

painPoints.label: Compliance Document Custody and Audit-Ready Retrieval
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: maintaining centralized, retrievable copies of compliance evidence for all teaching and licensed non-teaching staff — PRC certificates, CPD attestations, TDMS printouts, BED diplomas, subject certifications. Pain: documents are held by staff members personally or scattered across campus HR files. EXCLUDES: accreditation evidence (Fragmented Evidence Collection), HR personnel files (Workforce Management).

painPoints.label: No Centralized Audit Protocol or Schedule
painPoints.description: Multi-campus operators lack a formal internal audit schedule and consistent checklist across sites. Each campus may conduct its own spot checks, or audit is ad hoc when an issue surfaces. Pain: without a schedule, high-risk areas go unaudited for years. EXCLUDES: risk-based prioritisation methodology (Absence of Risk-Based Prioritisation), audit universe definition (Undefined Audit Universe).

painPoints.label: Audit Responsibility Diffused Across Roles
painPoints.description: Internal audit function is not centralized. Academic VP audits curriculum, CFO audits finance, HR audits staff records — no coordination. Pain: gaps between domains go unaudited, findings from one domain are not cross-referenced with another. EXCLUDES: audit protocol (No Centralized Audit Protocol), audit universe definition (Undefined Audit Universe).

painPoints.label: Audit Documentation and Evidence Trails
painPoints.description: Auditors visit campuses, make notes, identify findings, but lack standardized documentation. Pain: audit working papers are scattered (email, OneNote, handwritten notes), not retained in a central repository, making it impossible to defend audit quality or demonstrate completeness to external parties. EXCLUDES: finding follow-up (Finding Follow-up), protocol design (No Centralized Audit Protocol).

painPoints.label: Audit Finding Follow-up and Remediation Tracking
painPoints.description: Internal audits identify issues — payroll errors, cash handling gaps, facility hazards. Pain: responsibility for corrective action is assigned to campus managers, then lost. No follow-up mechanism exists. Findings re-appear in the next audit cycle. EXCLUDES: CAP for external audits (Accreditation/Inspection CAPs), protocol design.

painPoints.label: Absence of Risk-Based Audit Prioritization
painPoints.description: With 10-15 campuses and multiple compliance domains, 1-2 audit staff can't audit everything. Without risk-based prioritisation, effort is distributed equally. Pain: high-risk campuses or processes receive the same attention as low-risk ones. EXCLUDES: audit universe definition (Undefined Audit Universe), audit scheduling (No Centralized Audit Protocol).

painPoints.label: Management Response Delays and Uneven Rollout of Controls
painPoints.description: Even when audit findings are clear and CAPs formally tracked, implementation across 10-15 campuses is slow and uneven. Pain: approved controls are treated as optional by campus managers who face no consequence for delayed implementation. EXCLUDES: finding tracking (Finding Follow-up), audit scheduling.

painPoints.label: Undefined Audit Universe and Scope Criteria
painPoints.description: Multi-campus operators have no documented audit universe — a structured map of which processes, records, financial controls, and compliance domains are subject to internal audit at each campus, and at what frequency. EXCLUDES: risk-based prioritisation (Absence of Risk-Based Prioritisation), protocol design (No Centralized Audit Protocol).

painPoints.label: Auditor Independence and Domain Competency Gaps
painPoints.description: With 1-2 audit staff for 10-15 campuses, independence policies and competency requirements are rarely implemented. Pain: auditors are assigned to campuses where they have personal relationships; domain expertise gaps mean financial controls audits are superficial. EXCLUDES: audit staffing (Workforce Management), protocol design.

painPoints.label: Uncoordinated Campus Inspection Readiness
painPoints.description: DepEd, CHED, LGU, and accreditor inspections arrive with 1-2 weeks notice. Multi-campus operators receive notice centrally but must mobilise 10-15 campuses simultaneously. Pain: each campus has a different state of readiness; coordination happens ad hoc via WhatsApp/email; HQ has no visibility on which campuses are ready. EXCLUDES: document assembly (Document Assembly Bottleneck), staff interview preparation (Front-line Staff Interview Unpreparedness).

painPoints.label: Document Assembly and Verification Bottleneck
painPoints.description: Inspectors request curriculum maps, attendance records, teacher credentials, financial statements, facility maintenance logs. These live in various systems and locations. Pain: HQ scrambles to collect, verify, and assemble documents within 48-72 hours. Errors in assembled documents cause inspection findings. EXCLUDES: historical records retrieval (Incomplete Historical Records), accreditation evidence (Fragmented Evidence Collection).

painPoints.label: Interpretation Gap Between Inspectors and Operators
painPoints.description: During live inspections, inspectors use technical regulatory language that campus staff misinterpret. Pain: misinterpretation leads to committing to remediation that wasn't actually required, or failing to contest findings that were based on misapplied standards. EXCLUDES: staff interview preparation (Front-line Staff Interview Unpreparedness).

painPoints.label: Historical Finding Re-occurrence and Escalation
painPoints.description: When an inspector records a finding, the operator files a CAP and is expected to resolve it before the next cycle. Pain: CAP remediation is incomplete or undocumented; the same finding re-appears and escalates — from advisory to warning to possible suspension. EXCLUDES: CAP evidence insufficiency (CAP Evidence Insufficiency), internal audit finding follow-up (Internal Quality Audits).

painPoints.label: Corrective Action Evidence Insufficiency and Disputes
painPoints.description: After a finding, operators submit a CAP with evidence of remediation. Pain: evidence submitted is deemed insufficient by the regulator — wrong format, incomplete, or not tied to the specific finding standard. CAP is rejected and the cycle restarts. EXCLUDES: finding re-occurrence (Historical Finding Re-occurrence), accreditation CAP (Accreditation Corrective Action).

painPoints.label: Multi-Regulatory Coordination Friction
painPoints.description: Different inspecting bodies (DepEd, CHED, PRC, BIR, fire safety, accreditors) use different protocols, timelines, evidence formats, and standards. A finding by DepEd may require corrective action that conflicts with CHED requirements for the same function. EXCLUDES: accreditor interpretation variance (Accreditation Management), inter-agency data reconciliation (Regulatory Filings).

painPoints.label: Front-line Staff Inspector Interview Unpreparedness
painPoints.description: Inspectors routinely interview principals, registrars, subject coordinators, and faculty — not just the compliance officer. Pain: front-line staff across campuses are not consistently briefed on current regulatory expectations, give inconsistent or incorrect answers, or raise issues that wouldn't otherwise have been flagged. EXCLUDES: document assembly (Document Assembly Bottleneck), interpretation gap (Interpretation Gap).

painPoints.label: Policy Inconsistency and Version Control Across Campuses
painPoints.description: Multi-campus operators maintain policies in Word documents or PDFs on shared drives. Campuses may be operating on different policy versions. Pain: when an accreditor or inspector asks to see admissions policy, 5 different versions exist across campuses. EXCLUDES: staff awareness (Staff Policy Awareness), data retention policy (Data Retention and Disposal).

painPoints.label: Staff Policy Awareness and Training Compliance
painPoints.description: Policies are created centrally but compliance cannot demonstrate that campus staff have read and acknowledged them. Pain: no signed acknowledgment records exist — no policy attestation system. When asked by an accreditor, the operator cannot prove staff awareness. EXCLUDES: policy document management (Policy Inconsistency), CPD tracking (Teacher Registration & CPD).

painPoints.label: Data Retention and Disposal Protocols
painPoints.description: DepEd and the Philippine DPA require clear data retention schedules and secure disposal of student/staff personal data. Pain: most operators lack documented retention schedules; data is kept indefinitely or disposed of arbitrarily, creating DPA compliance risk. EXCLUDES: data governance architecture (Data & MIS), records management technical infrastructure (Data & MIS).

painPoints.label: Consent and Opt-Out Documentation Gaps
painPoints.description: DPA requires documented consent for processing personal data. Pain: most operators have no standardised consent forms. Parents/students are not explicitly asked for consent at enrolment. Opt-out mechanisms don't exist. EXCLUDES: data access controls (Data Access Audit Trail), breach notification (Data Breach Notification).

painPoints.label: Data Access Audit Trail and Compliance Visibility Gap
painPoints.description: DPA requires accountability for who accesses personal data and for what purpose. Pain: the compliance officer has no visibility into who has accessed student or staff data in the SIS, nor can they generate an audit log when required by the NPC. EXCLUDES: technical access control implementation (Data & MIS), IT security monitoring (Data & MIS).

painPoints.label: Data Breach Notification and NPC Reporting Failure
painPoints.description: DPA requires NPC notification within 72 hours of discovering a personal data breach. Pain: operators have no breach detection system, no notification template, and no defined protocol — if a breach occurs, the 72-hour window is typically missed, adding regulatory violation to the original breach. EXCLUDES: technical breach containment (Data & MIS), IT security incident response (Data & MIS).

painPoints.label: DPO Appointment and NPC Registration Non-Compliance
painPoints.description: NPC requires organisations with 250+ employees processing sensitive personal data to register and designate a DPO with sufficient authority. Pain: many operators have appointed a DPO in name only (assigned to existing HR/IT staff with no training, no authority, no budget), and NPC registration has not been completed or maintained. EXCLUDES: data processing activities (Data & MIS), staff training (Staff Policy Awareness).

painPoints.label: Third-Party Data Processor Management and Missing Data Sharing Agreements
painPoints.description: Operators engage SIS vendors, LMS providers, payment gateways, and HR platforms that process student/staff personal data. DPA requires Data Sharing Agreements (DSAs) with each. Pain: no DSA register exists; vendors have been onboarded without DPA clauses; the DPO has no visibility of all active data processors. EXCLUDES: vendor procurement (Finance/Procurement), IT vendor management (Data & MIS).

painPoints.label: Risk Register Exists on Paper, Not Actively Maintained
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: risk register created as a compliance artefact then shelved — risk ratings not updated when incidents occur, new risks not added during the year, responsible owners never review their risk items. Pain: the register bears no relationship to actual current risk state. EXCLUDES: risk appetite definition (Risk Appetite Undefined), business continuity planning (BCP Documentation).

painPoints.label: BCP Documentation Generic, Not Campus-Specific or Tested
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: BCP documents templated at HQ and distributed without localisation — campus-specific hazard profiles (flood zones, typhoon corridors, proximity to industrial sites) not incorporated; alternate site plans reference locations that don't exist; BCP never tested via tabletop or drill. EXCLUDES: facility emergency response (Facilities), crisis communication (No Defined Crisis Communication Chain).

painPoints.label: No Defined Crisis Communication Chain or Stakeholder Notification Protocol
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: during a crisis (campus fire, student fatality, typhoon displacement), there is no documented chain of command for communications — who calls whom, in what order, within what timeframe, with what message to parents/media/DepEd. Pain: messages are improvised, contradictory, or delayed. EXCLUDES: BCP documentation (BCP Documentation), NDRRMC coordination (NDRRMC Coordination).

painPoints.label: Insurance Coverage Gaps Discovered Only at Claims Time
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: property, liability, and employer insurance policies renewed annually without systematic coverage adequacy review — newly acquired campuses not enrolled, coverage limits not indexed to replacement cost, policy exclusions not checked against actual risk profile. EXCLUDES: insurance procurement (Finance), claims management (Finance).

painPoints.label: NDRRMC and LGU Coordination Is Reactive, Not Pre-Established
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: campuses have not pre-registered with DRRMO, have no MOUs with LGU evacuation centres, have no established contacts in NDRRMC/local emergency services before a crisis. Pain: coordination must be established from scratch during an emergency. EXCLUDES: BCP documentation (BCP Documentation), crisis communication (Crisis Communication Chain).

painPoints.label: Risk Appetite Undefined — Escalation Thresholds Left to Individual Judgement
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: board and executive team have not articulated a risk appetite statement — staff and campus principals do not know which risk categories require HQ escalation vs. local handling. Pain: serious incidents are managed locally without HQ awareness, or minor issues are unnecessarily escalated. EXCLUDES: risk register (Risk Register), crisis communication (Crisis Communication Chain).

painPoints.label: Child Protection Committee Non-Compliance and Unreported Incidents
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: DepEd DO 40-2012 obligations — Child Protection Committee (CPC) not constituted or inactive with no meeting minutes; mandatory reporting obligations for abuse/harassment not operationalised; campus staff do not know reporting thresholds or procedures; incidents handled informally and not documented. EXCLUDES: HR disciplinary process (Workforce Management), legal defence (Legal).

painPoints.label: Contractor and Vendor Risk Assessment Process Absent
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: no formal process for screening or periodically re-assessing contractors and vendors with campus access — school bus operators (driver licensing, vehicle roadworthiness), security guards (agency accreditation, NBI clearances), canteen concessionaires (food safety, health certifications), maintenance contractors (OSH compliance). EXCLUDES: vendor procurement (Finance), IT vendor management (Data & MIS).

painPoints.label: Multi-SIS Fragmentation & Manual Reconciliation
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the operational pain of managing 3-5 campus SIS instances with divergent schemas, user access configurations, and reporting structures acquired incrementally without consolidation — specifically: manual spreadsheet-based reconciliation of student records, cross-campus enrollment, and transfer credits; configuration drift between instances (different field mappings, grading scales, term structures); inability to surface a unified student record across sites; and the admin overhead of applying policy changes separately in each instance. EXCLUDES: ETL pipeline design or automated sync infrastructure between SIS instances (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines), master data standards and student record deduplication policy (Data Governance & Quality Management), major SIS consolidation or replacement projects (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle), first-line access and login issues (IT Service Desk & Platform Support).

painPoints.label: LMS Non-Utilization & Assessment Data Gaps
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the BAU operational consequence of low LMS utilization — specifically: assessment and attendance data remaining in paper gradebooks or isolated teacher spreadsheets rather than LMS (making it unavailable to SIS admin for reporting, compliance, and outcome tracking); configuration support requests from the minority of instructors who do use LMS outweighing available admin capacity; absence of usable learning outcome and formative assessment data in SIS/LMS systems despite platform being available; inability to generate standard reports on student progress because source data does not exist in any managed system. EXCLUDES: root cause analysis of why adoption failed — training programme design, incentive structures, and change management strategy are owned by Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle (Change Management & User Adoption Failures); decisions to procure or replace LMS platforms (Technology Change); curriculum design and pedagogy (Teaching & Learning).

painPoints.label: Transcript & Credential Generation Delays & Errors
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the operational pain of manual transcript production — specifically: registrar staff running multiple separate SIS queries and Excel cross-references to assemble a single transcript; 3-5 day turnaround for student transcript requests due to no automated generation workflow; errors in GPA calculation arising from retakes, incompletes, and late grade submissions not being propagated to the SIS before transcript generation; credential verification requests from employers and universities handled ad hoc with no standardised response process; absence of an automated degree-requirements completion check at the point of transcript generation. EXCLUDES: the upstream data quality root causes — grade reconciliation failures that produce incorrect source data are owned by Grading Data Silos & Assessment Reconciliation (sibling); master data duplication in student records (Data Governance & Quality Management); degree audit rules and academic policy (Teaching & Learning / academic registry); regulatory credential recognition and accreditation verification (Compliance & QA).

painPoints.label: Grading Data Silos & Assessment Reconciliation
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the operational pain of grade data being entered and maintained in multiple disconnected places — specifically: grades entered separately in LMS (Canvas/Moodle/Google Classroom), SIS, and paper or teacher-maintained spreadsheets with no automated reconciliation; end-of-term manual matching and spot-check process to produce a final grade set; late grades, incompletes, and retake results not automatically propagated to the SIS record; no single authoritative system of record for final marks, causing downstream errors in transcript generation and compliance reporting. EXCLUDES: the transcript production workflow itself (Transcript & Credential Generation — sibling child); grade appeal and student dispute resolution processes (academic affairs / student services); assessment design, grading policy, and marking rubrics (Teaching & Learning); compliance audit of grading records as a regulatory matter (Compliance & QA); ETL pipeline that would automate grade sync between LMS and SIS (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines).

painPoints.label: User Provisioning & Access Lifecycle Management
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the operational pain of managing SIS and LMS user account creation, role assignment, and deactivation across a multi-campus estate — specifically: bulk provisioning of student accounts at enrollment intake periods with no automated workflow, causing manual setup queues; staff role assignments in SIS/LMS not aligned to actual job function, causing over-permissioned or under-permissioned access; stale accounts remaining active after staff departure due to absent deprovisioning triggers; role proliferation across 3-5 campus SIS instances with no unified access policy; admin overhead of maintaining role templates separately per platform and campus. EXCLUDES: first-line access issue triage and password reset (IT Service Desk & Platform Support); HR system user records and employment status management (HR & People Operations); identity provider or SSO infrastructure configuration (IT Security or Systems Integration); cybersecurity access policy enforcement (IT Security, DR & Business Continuity).

painPoints.label: Multi-Instance SIS Patch Coordination & Version Drift
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the operational pain of managing minor and patch releases across 3-5 campus SIS instances that are on divergent version states — specifically: no coordinated patch schedule, causing some campuses to be multiple minor versions behind; patch releases applied at one campus breaking LMS grade passback or SIS-to-SIS data sync integrations that were working on older versions; absence of a test environment for patch validation before production deployment; vendor support responses premised on a single-instance deployment model, providing no guidance for multi-instance coordination; admin staff spending unplanned time on reactive patch rollback and re-testing. EXCLUDES: major version upgrades requiring formal UAT and change management (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle); integration infrastructure and pipeline fixes triggered by version changes (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines); hardware and network infrastructure at campuses (Site & Facilities Operations); vendor contract renegotiation or vendor selection (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle).

painPoints.label: Absent Master Data Management (MDM) for Students & Staff
painPoints.description: No golden record for student or staff identity. Same person exists under multiple IDs across SIS, LMS, finance, and HR systems (e.g., Juan Cruz, Juan C. Cruz, J. Cruz). Deduplication is manual and retroactive. Merges corrupt historical records. Impossible to track a student's complete journey across enrollments, transfers, and outcomes across campuses. EXCLUDES: technical batch reconciliation and ETL deduplication overhead (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines); regulatory reporting gaps from incomplete data linkage (Compliance Audit Readiness & Regulatory Reporting Gaps).

painPoints.label: Undetected Data Quality Incidents & Silent Corruption
painPoints.description: No systematic data validation at point of entry by staff or system forms (e.g., age < 5 enrolled, birthdate in future, negative payment amounts accepted, impossible grade values submitted). Errors created at source accumulate silently in BAU operations. Discovered late during month-end or term-end reporting — too late to fix retroactively. Manual fixes create inconsistency across records. No audit trail of corrections. High manual effort to trace root cause and impact scope when errors are eventually found. EXCLUDES: data corruption caused by pipeline errors, ETL join failures, or integration bugs (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines); errors discovered during regulatory audits (Compliance Audit Readiness & Regulatory Reporting Gaps).

painPoints.label: Compliance Audit Readiness & Regulatory Reporting Gaps
painPoints.description: No audit trail of data changes — cannot answer 'who changed this record, when, and from what value' for any data domain. When DepEd, CHED, PRC, or BIR audits are triggered, teams scramble to manually compile reports from incompatible systems because no unified, traceable data lineage exists. Instructor-subject-cohort assignments are overwritten without history. Enrollment changes leave no version history. Accreditation visits expose inability to reconstruct data state at a prior point in time. Reconciliation between submitted reports and live system data reveals unexplained discrepancies with no traceability. Audit trail incompleteness is discovered under deadline pressure, with no time for remediation. EXCLUDES: regulatory content requirements, retention schedules, and compliance obligations (Compliance & QA); financial reporting and reconciliation (Finance & Revenue); identity deduplication and record linkage failures (Absent Master Data Management).

painPoints.label: Undefined Retention & Purge Policies, Data Sprawl
painPoints.description: No formal data retention schedule or data classification framework. Inactive student records, old transcripts, and legacy employee data remain in live systems indefinitely. Databases bloat; backup and recovery times increase. No classification labels on database objects — production archives are misidentified as test data and deleted in cleanup operations, causing permanent loss of required archival records. Unclear authority to delete: registrar, IT, and legal each disclaim ownership. Privacy risk: old personal data not purged per DPA/NPC consent obligations. Occasional panic cleanups introduce errors or destroy records that were legally required to be retained. EXCLUDES: regulatory retention schedule requirements and compliance obligations (Compliance & QA); backup, restore, and disaster recovery operations (IT Security, DR & Business Continuity); physical storage and filing systems (Site & Facilities Operations).

painPoints.label: Undefined Data Ownership & Stewardship Accountability
painPoints.description: No documented data owner for any data domain, field, or system of record. When departments disagree on a data value or definition (e.g., which system holds the authoritative student_status), there is no named steward with decision-making authority and no escalation path. Data governance issues surface as inter-departmental disputes with no resolution mechanism. Ownership vacuums cause change-freeze: no one will approve a field definition change without sign-off that no one has authority to give. Data quality remediation requests are bounced between IT, registrar, and finance without resolution. EXCLUDES: technical data pipelines and integration ownership (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines); platform configuration authority (SIS & LMS Admin).

painPoints.label: Absent Data Dictionary & Cross-Department Definitional Conflicts
painPoints.description: No shared data dictionary defining authoritative meanings for key operational terms. 'Active student' means enrolled (registrar), paying (finance), or recently logged in (LMS) depending on who is asked — producing three different enrollment counts for the same period. 'Completion' is defined differently by the registrar (unit completion), DepEd (attendance threshold), and CHED (credential conferral). These definitional gaps produce persistent discrepancies across reports, board packs, and regulatory submissions with no mechanism to resolve which definition is authoritative. New hires and analysts must learn informal interpretations through tribal knowledge. Downstream: MDM deduplication, data quality rules, and compliance reports all depend on agreed definitions that do not exist. EXCLUDES: regulatory definition requirements imposed by DepEd/CHED (Compliance & QA); system-level field naming conventions (SIS & LMS Admin).

painPoints.label: Manual Report Assembly & End-of-Period Crunch
painPoints.description: Monthly, quarterly, and annual reporting requires teams to export from SIS, LMS, and finance system into separate Excel files, then consolidate manually. Cross-system reconciliation is manual (e.g., billed vs. enrolled headcount). 5-7 days of concentrated effort at month-end, prone to formula errors and version conflicts. Late discovery of discrepancies. No automated historical trend analysis. Campus principals lack timely visibility into enrollment, drop rates, or academic performance until 2-3 weeks post-period. EXCLUDES: root-cause data quality remediation and master data fixes (Data Governance & Quality Management), ETL and pipeline architecture solutions (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines).

painPoints.label: Missing Real-Time Operational Dashboards
painPoints.description: No self-service dashboards exist for campus principals, deans, or department heads to track operational KPIs in real-time (enrollment by program, cost per student, graduation rates, failure rates by course). Business intelligence tools are not deployed or are underutilised due to underlying data quality issues. Decisions are made on outdated reports or gut feel, not live data. HR does not see attrition risk signals until exit interviews. EXCLUDES: BI tool procurement, vendor selection, and platform deployment decisions (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle), data quality remediation that would enable reliable dashboards (Data Governance & Quality Management), report consumer training and self-service adoption (see: Report Consumer Literacy & Self-Service Adoption Gap).

painPoints.label: Student Outcome Tracking & Learning Analytics Absent
painPoints.description: No systematic tracking of learning outcomes post-graduation (job placement, further study, employer feedback). Formative assessment data is trapped in LMS or paper records and is not aggregated for analysis. The operator cannot answer: 'Are graduates meeting industry-ready competencies?' or 'Which courses correlate with low pass rates?' No dedicated institutional research analyst or function exists. Accreditation bodies require outcome evidence; the operator's inability to produce it is a symptom of absent tracking infrastructure — not a compliance process gap. EXCLUDES: regulatory outcome reporting submissions and accreditation filings (Compliance & QA), alumni engagement or career placement programmes (Student Services, if any), LMS configuration that would surface assessment data (SIS & LMS Administration).

painPoints.label: Finance & Enrollment Reporting Misalignment
painPoints.description: Management information packs and operational reports present contradictory enrollment and revenue figures with no resolution path for decision-makers. Campus principals and executives cannot tell which figure is authoritative: finance-system revenue and SIS enrollment counts produce inconsistent implied per-student rates, and these conflicts are unresolved at the point of reporting. This forces leadership to make resourcing and planning decisions against numbers they do not trust. The reporting function surfaces the problem but has no mechanism to arbitrate or correct it. EXCLUDES: root-cause MDM and student-record identity resolution (Data Governance & Quality Management), ETL and batch reconciliation pipeline fixes (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines), bad debt collection processes and aging management (Finance & Revenue), government funding compliance and revenue recognition accounting (Finance & Revenue and Compliance & QA).

painPoints.label: Adhoc Reporting Request Backlog & Slow Turnaround
painPoints.description: The data team (1-2 people) is overwhelmed with ad-hoc reporting requests from leadership, compliance, and HR (e.g., 'How many students from Visayas are enrolled in nursing?', 'What is faculty utilisation rate this semester?'). Each request requires 2-4 hours of manual query writing and Excel assembly. The queue grows week-to-week. Strategic analysis requests are de-prioritised or abandoned. Leadership cannot quickly test hypotheses or respond to market opportunities because data retrieval is a bottleneck, not a capability. EXCLUDES: strategic analytics infrastructure investment decisions (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle), self-service training and adoption (see: Report Consumer Literacy & Self-Service Adoption Gap).

painPoints.label: No Standard KPI Definitions or Network-Wide Metric Catalogue
painPoints.description: Different campuses define and count the same metrics differently (e.g., 'enrollment' counted as deposit-paid at some sites, first-attendance at others; 'completion rate' defined inconsistently across programs). No canonical KPI library or data dictionary exists to anchor reporting. Managers dispute what numbers mean before they can question whether the numbers are accurate. Network-wide benchmarking reports aggregate incompatible definitions, producing misleading cross-site comparisons. Leadership cannot confidently act on comparative data until definitional conflicts are resolved. EXCLUDES: technical master data and identity resolution (Data Governance & Quality Management), KPI reporting tool configuration (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle).

painPoints.label: Report Consumer Literacy & Self-Service Adoption Gap
painPoints.description: Campus principals, deans, and department heads lack the data literacy to use self-service reporting tools or interpret operational dashboards without analyst support. Training is absent or delivered once at go-live and not reinforced. Adoption of BI tools collapses within months of deployment as managers revert to requesting manual reports. The reporting function becomes a permanent bottleneck rather than an enabler of distributed decision-making. At 500-staff multi-site scale, this is a standing operational pain-point, not a one-time change management problem. EXCLUDES: BI tool procurement and platform selection (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle), underlying data quality issues that undermine dashboard reliability (Data Governance & Quality Management), general staff digital literacy for non-reporting tools (IT Service Desk & Platform Support).

painPoints.label: Point-to-Point Integrations & No ETL Infrastructure
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the steady-state operational failure mode of point-to-point integrations — SIS, LMS, Finance, HR, and Parent Portal systems connected via manual exports/imports or brittle custom scripts with no centralized ETL or integration platform. When SIS schema changes, dependent systems break without warning. Nightly batch jobs fail silently; failures discovered reactively (next-day staff meeting, user complaint). Data latency 24+ hours for operational processes (e.g., new enrollment not visible in finance system until next morning; grade post not reflected in reporting until following day). No event-driven or webhook-based triggers for time-sensitive workflows. No centralized logging or monitoring of integration health. EXCLUDES: connectivity-driven sync failures at offline/low-connectivity campuses (Offline & Low-Connectivity Campus Data Sync Failures), change-event-triggered cascade breakage when system fields or schemas are modified (Change Velocity Bottleneck & Integration Testing Gaps).

painPoints.label: Duplicate Source Systems & Batch Reconciliation Overhead
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the pipeline and reconciliation overhead caused by multi-source data entry — student data entered independently in SIS and enrollment portal, HR data maintained in both payroll system and HR information system, finance data in accounting software and manual campus ledgers. Operators spend 15–20 hours/month running cross-system reconciliation jobs to align records and correct mismatches. Offline campuses create a third copy (local spreadsheet), producing three-way split scenarios on reconnection. No automated deduplication or merge pipeline exists. EXCLUDES: the policy question of which system is the authoritative master record and why (master data definitions and authoritative-record governance owned by Data Governance & Quality Management), data quality standards and cleansing rules (Data Governance & Quality Management), financial reconciliation of transaction records (Finance & Revenue).

painPoints.label: Offline & Low-Connectivity Campus Data Sync Failures
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: data synchronisation failures caused by unreliable or absent internet connectivity at remote and provincial campuses. When connectivity drops for hours or days, SIS, enrollment, and grading systems become inaccessible; local staff revert to offline Excel copies and manual paper records. On reconnection, sync conflicts, duplicate records, and data loss occur because no offline-first or conflict-resolution protocol exists. Central systems can be out of sync for weeks. Critical processes (enrollment confirmation, grade submission) are delayed until connectivity is restored, causing knock-on delays in finance, reporting, and compliance. EXCLUDES: remote IT support staffing and service desk coverage (IT Service Desk & Platform Support — the inability to provide remote technical support is context for why sync issues escalate, not a Systems Integration pain), data quality policy for offline-generated records (Data Governance & Quality Management), physical connectivity infrastructure (Site & Facilities Operations).

painPoints.label: API Governance & Third-Party Integration Risk
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: absence of formal API contracts and integration specifications between internal systems and third-party vendors (LMS, payroll, parent portal, tuition payment gateway). No version control or change-log for integration code; scripts live on individual staff laptops. No documented API deprecation process — vendors change or retire endpoints without notice, causing silent integration failures. API rate limits undocumented; bulk operations fail unexpectedly. No central integration registry listing active connections, owners, or SLAs. Bus-factor risk: integration knowledge concentrated in one or two staff members with no documented handover. EXCLUDES: API credential security policy, secrets management, and key-rotation enforcement (IT Security, DR & Business Continuity — see Access Control & PII Protection Sprawl), physical network security (Site & Facilities Operations).

painPoints.label: Change Velocity Bottleneck & Integration Testing Gaps
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the technical integration debt that makes every system change risky and slow — adding a field to SIS (e.g., 'scholarship status') requires manual, untested updates to 3–4 dependent downstream systems and reports. No automated integration test suite; regression testing done ad-hoc by operators. Cascade breakage is discovered in production, requiring manual data corrections that take hours to days. Rollback is complex because no integration staging environment exists. Schema changes, new data fields, or report format updates all carry high breakage risk, creating a chilling effect on legitimate improvement work. EXCLUDES: project pipeline prioritisation, technology demand governance, and strategic sequencing of change initiatives (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle), change management and user adoption planning (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle), capex approval for integration tooling (Finance & Revenue).

painPoints.label: Integration Monitoring Blindspot & Reactive-Only Failure Detection
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: the absence of any integration health monitoring, pipeline observability, or proactive alerting across all data flows between SIS, LMS, Finance, HR, and third-party systems. No dashboard exists showing which pipelines are running, when they last succeeded, or how long they took. Failed batch jobs are discovered reactively — through user complaints, end-of-day discrepancies, or next-morning staff meetings — rather than through automated alerts. No SLA tracking for integration pipelines (e.g., 'enrollment must appear in finance within 2 hours'). No ability to distinguish transient failures (retry-safe) from persistent failures (requiring manual intervention). IT and data staff spend significant time on reactive incident investigation that could be eliminated with basic observability. EXCLUDES: integration code architecture and ETL infrastructure (Point-to-Point Integrations & No ETL Infrastructure), IT infrastructure and network monitoring (Site & Facilities Operations), application-layer uptime monitoring (IT Service Desk & Platform Support).

painPoints.label: No Technology Roadmap or Strategic Alignment
painPoints.description: Technology decisions reactive: 'We need an LMS, let's buy one.' No linked business strategy (e.g., digital transformation, outcome tracking, financial sustainability). No multi-year technology investment plan — budget scattered across one-off, unsequenced projects (50K for new laptop lab, 100K for LMS) without an integrated vision or prioritisation rationale. Conflicting priorities across campuses — each principal wants different systems, no network-wide alignment. IT unable to plan multi-year investments or workforce capacity. EXCLUDES: vendor selection process and contractual outcomes (vendor-lock-in node); project execution failures once a project is approved and underway (budget-overruns-delays node).

painPoints.label: Change Management & User Adoption Failures
painPoints.description: New systems implemented without a change management plan. Users not trained or involved in design. Go-live delayed or rollout incomplete due to inadequate change readiness — stakeholder resistance, training skipped during school term, no communications plan. Parallel running (old + new system) extends 6+ months because users refuse to adopt the new system, costing double. User resistance sustained; staff revert to old ways. System adoption metrics not tracked; projects declared 'complete' with 40% active utilisation. Investment wasted on a functioning system that nobody uses. Leadership blames IT; IT blames users. EXCLUDES: timeline slippage and cost overruns caused by scope creep or integration complexity (budget-overruns-delays node); ongoing low utilisation of a platform that was successfully launched (SIS & LMS Admin — LMS Platform Underutilization).

painPoints.label: Project Budget Overruns, Timeline Slippage & Scope Creep
painPoints.description: Projects scoped informally ('rough estimate: 6 months, 500K'). No change control process. Scope creeps mid-project (e.g., 'while we're at it, let's add payroll integration'). Budget exhausted before completion. Vendor change orders not negotiated; costs balloon. Timeline slips 3–6 months due to underestimated integration complexity, undiscovered legacy data issues, or uncontrolled scope additions — not change-readiness or adoption failures. Leadership loses confidence in IT; critical projects de-prioritised or cancelled. EXCLUDES: slippage caused by user resistance or inadequate training (change-management-absent node); portfolio-level budget scatter and absence of a multi-year investment plan (no-roadmap node).

painPoints.label: Vendor Lock-In & Proprietary Integration Risk
painPoints.description: System selected without data portability, open API standards, or a documented exit strategy. Switching costs prohibitive (e.g., rewriting all proprietary integrations). Vendor features limited; operator cannot extend without expensive, slow vendor-led development. Multi-year contracts lock in features, pricing, and terms with little renegotiation leverage. Dissatisfaction grows but switching risk is too high. Operator feels trapped — unable to upgrade, pivot to a better product, or respond to network growth. EXCLUDES: day-to-day API integration maintenance and third-party connector risk (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines — API Governance node); vendor selection decision process and criteria failure (no-roadmap node).

painPoints.label: Technology Demand Management & Project Prioritisation Failure
painPoints.description: No governance model for receiving, evaluating, and sequencing technology change requests. Campus principals and functional heads submit competing requests ad hoc; IT has no structured intake process. No criteria for assessing business value, urgency, or strategic alignment. COO arbitrates project approvals based on loudest voice or last request. IT capacity over-allocated; work-in-progress capped only by IT exhaustion. Projects started without formal approval, then abandoned mid-flight. Operators with 5+ campuses experience chronic under-delivery: 12 projects in flight, none fully complete. EXCLUDES: strategic roadmap absence (no-roadmap node); project execution failures once a project is formally underway (budget-overruns-delays node).

painPoints.label: Platform Retirement Failures & Technical Debt Accumulation
painPoints.description: Legacy systems never formally decommissioned after replacement. Old platforms kept running in parallel — duplicated licences paid, shadow integrations maintained, staff using both old and new systems creating data inconsistency. No formal retirement schedule, no migration completion gate, no decommission checklist. Shadow IT accumulates: campus-level tools, personal databases, ad-hoc integrations built outside IT governance. Technical debt compounds: old integrations fragile but too risky to touch; new system can't fully replace legacy because data migration never completed. Annual cost of maintaining dead systems untracked. EXCLUDES: active project implementation and go-live delivery (covered by other nodes in this branch); SaaS subscription sprawl for non-project tools (IT Service Desk — SaaS Licence Inventory node).

painPoints.label: Requirements Gathering & Business Case Failures
painPoints.description: Technology projects initiated without written requirements or a validated business case. IT builds solutions based on informal conversations; academic operations and functional stakeholders discover misalignment at UAT. Projects restart or are significantly reworked post-build. Business cases not written, so cost-benefit rationale cannot be reviewed or learned from. Stakeholders disagree at scope definition: IT, principals, and academic operations have different understandings of what the system must do. Requirements creep because initial scope was never documented. Projects proceed to procurement and vendor selection before anyone has confirmed what the system must deliver. EXCLUDES: scope creep and change orders during active project delivery (budget-overruns-delays node); strategic portfolio alignment and investment planning (no-roadmap node).

painPoints.label: Access Request Ticket Storm from Broken JML Workflow
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: high-volume IT service desk ticket burden generated by broken joiner/mover/leaver (JML) workflow — new staff arrive with no system access configured, generating same-day access request tickets; batch account creation via HR spreadsheets fails silently, producing ghost accounts or locked-out users that generate follow-up tickets; role inheritance is undefined (campus admin sees all students network-wide rather than their campus cohort only), generating repeated mis-access complaints; orphaned accounts from departed staff accumulate and generate security-flag tickets when detected; no automated deprovisioning means IT manually closes accounts weeks after exit, consuming recurring capacity. EXCLUDES: role design and access control policy definition (SIS & LMS Admin), security posture assessment and compliance obligations for orphaned account risk (IT Security, DR & Business Continuity), HR employment records and exit workflow ownership (HR & People Operations), first-line SIS/LMS configuration for role setup (SIS & LMS Admin).

painPoints.label: No SLA Framework & Chronic Ticket Backlog
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: absence of formal SLA tiers (P1/P2/P3) for IT incidents across campuses; service desk staffed by 1-2 generalists handling 50-150 tickets/week with no priority queue; critical issues (enrollment system down on enrolment day, exam grade submission failures) treated same as low-priority requests (email signature change); no escalation matrix; tickets resolved in order received; no on-call or after-hours coverage for P1 incidents occurring outside business hours — critical platform failures on evenings and weekends go unresponded until next business day. EXCLUDES: DR and major outage response (IT Security, DR & Business Continuity), SIS/LMS configuration fixes (SIS & LMS Admin), outage status communication to affected staff network-wide (see Incident Communication & Status Broadcast Absence).

painPoints.label: Multi-Site Remote Support Coverage Gaps
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: service desk staff physically located at head office or flagship campus; provincial and remote campuses (e.g., Visayas, Mindanao sites) have no on-site IT support; remote troubleshooting hampered by low connectivity; campus staff resolve issues by rebooting or waiting; critical platform outages at remote sites go unresolved for 24-72 hours; time zone and language barriers (Cebuano, Ilocano) add friction. EXCLUDES: physical hardware repair and local LAN infrastructure (Site & Facilities Operations), connectivity provisioning (Site & Facilities Operations).

painPoints.label: SaaS Licence Inventory & Subscription Sprawl
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: no central register of SaaS subscriptions across campuses; duplicate subscriptions purchased independently by individual campuses for the same tool (e.g., three separate Zoom EDU accounts); unused licences not deprovisioned after staff turnover; auto-renewal of abandoned subscriptions undetected; finance pays invoices for tools IT does not know are active; network-wide licence optimisation not performed; no consolidated view of SaaS spend or seat utilisation across the operator network. EXCLUDES: capex hardware procurement (Site & Facilities Operations), major platform selection and vendor evaluation (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle), vendor SLA performance tracking and contract enforcement (IT Service Desk — Vendor Escalation Dead-Ends node).

painPoints.label: Slow IT Onboarding & Offboarding for Staff Joiners/Leavers
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: no automated joiner/mover/leaver (JML) workflow integrated with HR; new faculty or admin staff wait 3-10 days for email accounts, system access, and device setup to be completed; critical start-day blockers (no LMS access, cannot access grading system on first day of classes); departing staff accounts not deactivated for weeks after exit, leaving active credentials in live systems; manual handover of IT assets undocumented; IT chasing HR for approval, HR chasing IT for completion. EXCLUDES: HR contracts and employment records (HR & People Operations), access control policy definition (SIS & LMS Admin for role design).

painPoints.label: Vendor Escalation Dead-Ends & SLA Non-Enforcement
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: when third-party platform issues escalate beyond first-line (e.g., SaaS portal bug, payment gateway downtime, productivity suite outage), the service desk has no formal escalation path or account manager contact; vendor response times exceed contractual SLA but operator has no mechanism to enforce or claim credits; unresolved vendor tickets sit for weeks with no follow-up; operators lack contractual leverage due to small account size relative to vendor; no vendor performance scorecards maintained. EXCLUDES: vendor evaluation and contract negotiation for new systems (Technology Change & Platform Lifecycle), SIS/LMS vendor escalation for configuration issues (SIS & LMS Admin).

painPoints.label: Absent Knowledge Base & Repeat-Incident Rework
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: no documented solutions for recurring IT issues (e.g., 'SIS login error on Chrome — clear cache' resolved 40x per term with no fix article); service desk staff solve the same problems from scratch each time; institutional IT knowledge held in individual staff heads, lost on resignation; no self-service portal for campus staff to resolve common issues; training new IT hires takes 2-3 months because no runbooks exist; manual workarounds for known bugs never codified. EXCLUDES: SIS/LMS configuration runbooks (SIS & LMS Admin), cybersecurity incident response playbooks (IT Security, DR & Business Continuity).

painPoints.label: Shadow IT & Unsanctioned SaaS Proliferation at Campus Level
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: campus staff independently signing up for free or freemium SaaS tools (Google Forms for student data collection, WhatsApp for operational coordination, Notion for tracking, unapproved file-sharing platforms) because IT Service Desk response is too slow or inaccessible; these tools are unknown to IT and never registered in any licence or asset inventory; student and staff data processed in unsanctioned tools outside data governance policy and PDPA compliance controls; shadow IT estate discovered only reactively (e.g., breach notification, CHED audit, staff departure); no campus-level IT liaison or approved self-service catalogue to redirect demand to sanctioned alternatives. EXCLUDES: known SaaS subscriptions with missing central register (SaaS Licence Inventory & Subscription Sprawl), data classification and retention policy enforcement for discovered tools (Data Governance & Quality Management), PDPA regulatory compliance breach notification (Compliance & QA).

painPoints.label: No Incident Communication Channel or Status Broadcast Capability
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: when a platform outage occurs, no mechanism exists to broadcast incident status to 500+ staff across 22 sites — no status page, no IT incident notification list, no SMS/push alert channel; affected users have no way to know if a system is down or if they are experiencing a local issue, generating a secondary flood of 'is the system working?' tickets that overwhelms the already understaffed desk; staff and campus coordinators resort to informal WhatsApp groups for outage information; no on-call contact published for after-hours P1 incidents; no standard incident update cadence (e.g., update every 30 min during active outage). EXCLUDES: DR and business continuity response for major infrastructure failures (IT Security, DR & Business Continuity), SLA enforcement and ticket prioritisation (No SLA Framework & Chronic Ticket Backlog), root-cause investigation and platform restoration (relevant platform owner).

painPoints.label: BC Governance Blindspots & Leadership Exposure
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: absence of board- and leadership-level visibility into IT recovery risk — no documented risk register entry for IT system failure, no scenario-based executive briefing on 3-7 day SIS/finance outage impact; insurance policy not reviewed against actual recovery capability (underwriter coverage gaps); regulatory audit findings on DR readiness not escalated to leadership; no formal annual BCP sign-off process for IT-dependent systems; staff and campus principals unaware of degraded-mode operating procedures. EXCLUDES: technical backup artifact gaps and restore testing (Backup Coverage Gaps & Untested Restore Integrity), cyber incident playbooks and response roles (No Cyber Incident Response Plan), campus-differentiated RTO/RPO targeting (DR Plan Not Differentiated by Campus), enterprise BCP governance and regulatory crisis protocols (Compliance & QA).

painPoints.label: RBAC Framework Absence & Access Review Gaps
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: absence of a formally defined role-based access control (RBAC) policy — role definitions, permission matrices, and access tiers not documented for SIS, finance system, HR system, or LMS; separation-of-duties violations not identified or remediated (e.g. finance staff seeing payroll equivalents, campus admins seeing all-campus student PII); no periodic access review or recertification process; no PII masking or data subsetting in non-production environments; system access logs not reviewed for anomalous access; no MFA enforcement policy — MFA not mandated for privileged or PII-access roles on any system. EXCLUDES: user provisioning and de-provisioning execution workflows (IT Service Desk — User Provisioning & Access Management Chaos), identity verification at the help desk (IT Service Desk), data classification and PII policy definition (Data Governance & Quality Management), PDPA compliance obligations and breach notification (Compliance & QA), turnover-driven offboarding process delays (IT Service Desk — Slow IT Onboarding & Offboarding).

painPoints.label: No Formal Vulnerability Management or Patch Cadence
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: absence of a structured process to identify, prioritise, and remediate known software and firmware vulnerabilities across the network (servers, SaaS tenants, campus-connected devices); unpatched operating systems and application versions; no scheduled patching cadence or emergency patch SLA; vulnerability scan results not reviewed or acted on; campus IT staff applying patches ad-hoc or not at all; exploit exposure accumulating between annual reviews. EXCLUDES: physical device procurement and hardware refresh (Site & Facilities Operations), vendor patch release decisions (external).

painPoints.label: No Cyber Incident Response Plan or Tested Playbooks
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: absence of a documented cyber incident response plan covering ransomware, credential theft, data exfiltration, and phishing compromise; no defined roles, escalation paths, or decision authority during a live incident; incident detection relies on end-user reports rather than automated alerting; post-incident forensic capability absent; no tabletop exercises or red-team drills conducted; breach notification handoff to Compliance & QA undefined in practice. EXCLUDES: regulatory breach notification (Compliance & QA), physical security response (Site & Facilities Operations).

painPoints.label: Backup Coverage Gaps & Untested Restore Integrity
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: backup schedules covering some but not all critical systems (SIS, finance, LMS, HR); backups stored on-site only with no offsite or cloud copy; backup jobs completing with errors not monitored or alerted; restore procedures never tested — operators discover failures only during actual recovery; RPO and RTO targets not formally defined per system criticality; manual backup processes dependent on individual IT staff remembering to run jobs. EXCLUDES: physical storage hardware (Site & Facilities Operations), data retention policy definition (Data Governance & Quality Management).

painPoints.label: High Phishing & Social Engineering Exposure Across Campuses
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: faculty and admin staff with no security awareness training clicking phishing links or disclosing credentials; Business Email Compromise (BEC) targeting finance approvers; credential stuffing attacks on SIS and parent portals exploiting weak or reused passwords; MFA absence on email, SIS, and financial systems creating an accessible attack surface (MFA enforcement policy gap owned by RBAC Framework Absence & Access Review Gaps); IT help desk social engineering — password reset without identity verification; high staff turnover amplifying credential hygiene gaps; no simulated phishing campaign or security culture measurement programme. EXCLUDES: PDPA compliance training (Compliance & QA), MFA enforcement policy definition and RBAC access control (RBAC Framework Absence & Access Review Gaps), email platform procurement and SaaS subscription management (IT Service Desk).

painPoints.label: DR Plan Not Differentiated by Campus Connectivity & Criticality
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: single network-level DR plan applied uniformly to campuses with vastly different infrastructure maturity — provincial campuses with satellite internet treated identically to Manila flagship; no campus-tier classification (critical, standard, minimal) driving differentiated RTO/RPO targets; DR rehearsals conducted only at headquarters, not at regional or remote sites; no degraded-mode operating procedures for campuses that lose central system access; IT resources insufficient to support simultaneous recovery across 10-20 sites. EXCLUDES: campus physical infrastructure resilience (Site & Facilities Operations), enterprise-wide BCP governance (Compliance & QA).

painPoints.label: Unmanaged Third-Party & SaaS Security Risk
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: SaaS vendors (LMS, tuition portals, HR tools) holding student and staff PII without formal security assessment or contractual data processing agreements; no vendor security questionnaire or periodic security review process; SaaS tenants configured with default security settings (no MFA enforcement, overly permissive sharing); shadow IT — campus-level SaaS subscriptions procured without central IT visibility; no process to offboard vendor access when contracts end; data residency unknown for offshore SaaS providers relative to PDPA requirements. EXCLUDES: PDPA data processing agreement negotiation and legal review (Compliance & QA), SaaS contract renewal, SLA monitoring, and vendor escalation (IT Service Desk), API integration governance and pipeline security posture for system-to-system integrations (Systems Integration & Data Pipelines), SaaS licence inventory and subscription cost management (IT Service Desk).

painPoints.label: Flat Network Architecture & Lateral Movement Risk
painPoints.description: INCLUDES: absence of network segmentation between campuses and between internal network zones — student, staff, admin, finance, and operations traffic traversing the same flat LAN/VLAN; no firewall policy enforcement between campus segments; a single compromised endpoint or campus device able to reach SIS, finance, and HR systems directly via the internal network; no network-level controls isolating guest Wi-Fi from administrative systems; inter-campus VPN or SD-WAN not segmented by data sensitivity; campus IT switches and routers using default credentials or unpatched firmware; no network traffic monitoring or east-west detection capability. EXCLUDES: physical network cabling and hardware procurement (Site & Facilities Operations), WAN/internet connectivity provisioning (Site & Facilities Operations), vulnerability patching cadence for network devices (No Formal Vulnerability Management or Patch Cadence — covers patch scheduling; this node covers the architectural segmentation gap).

painPoints.label: Fragmented Alumni Data Across Campuses
painPoints.description: Includes: alumni records scattered across 10-15 independent campus databases with no unified master ID or master list; inconsistent naming conventions and ID schemas across campuses; difficulty reconciling duplicate records when alumni attend multiple campuses or transfer; inability to produce a clean, deduplicated alumni list for CHED tracer study submissions or accreditation reports. Excludes: the human effort of performing reconciliation work (Manual Data Reconciliation & Entry Burden); student-to-alumni record migration at the moment of graduation; individual data privacy concerns.

painPoints.label: Contact Information Decay & Lost Connectivity
painPoints.description: Includes: alumni moving domestically and internationally without updated contact details, phone number portability issues in Philippines telecommunications, no systematic update mechanism (email bounces, SMS fails), inability to reach 40-60% of cohorts after 3 years. Excludes: GDPR/privacy compliance for contact retention, data minimization.

painPoints.label: Outcome Tracking Blind Spots
painPoints.description: Includes: no structured mechanism to track employment status (employed/self-employed/further study/overseas/abroad â€” including OFW placements, which represent a major outcome segment in PH graduate cohorts); salary and compensation ranges; sector and industry placement; timeline to first job; no distinction between aspirational and actual outcomes reported by alumni. Excludes: graduate satisfaction surveys and qualitative career satisfaction; board exam passage rates and PRC licensure data (Board Exam Results Reconciliation); CHED tracer study compliance format and submission obligations (CHED Tracer Study Compliance Burden).

painPoints.label: Manual Data Reconciliation & Entry Burden
painPoints.description: Includes: staff manually consolidating outcome data from alumni questionnaires, LinkedIn, employer confirmation requests, and CHED tracer form responses with no automated matching; repeated manual data entry across the enrollment system, alumni tracking database, and CHED reporting templates; no workflow tooling to route, assign, or track completion of alumni follow-up tasks. Excludes: the data architecture state (fragmented schemas, missing master IDs) that causes the work â€” covered in Fragmented Alumni Data; employer relationship CRM (Industry & Employer Partnerships); automation tooling strategy or procurement decisions.

painPoints.label: No Accountability for Outcome Claims
